title: High-Q cryogenic surface acoustic wave resonators in the GHz range
authors: Aldo Tarascio, Oliver Wicki, Dominik M. Zumbühl
abstract: Surface acoustic wave (SAW) resonators provide a compact platform for confining microwave-frequency phonons and are widely used in radio-frequency technologies, but their operation at gigahertz frequencies and cryogenic temperatures remains challenging. In this regime, conventional design rules do not directly apply, and achieving high-quality acoustic confinement requires careful consideration about geometry and loss mechanisms. Here, we present a systematic experimental study of SAW resonators on gallium arsenide, a platform of particular interest for hybrid quantum devices but comparatively unexplored for high-Q SAW cavities. By varying key design parameters such as cavity length, wavelength, and crystal orientation, we study resonator performance and achieve quality factors up to 28000 in the gigahertz range. In addition, we introduce mesa steps within the acoustic cavity, mimicking realistic device architectures and providing insight into scattering processes and additional dissipation channels. Our results establish practical design guidelines for GaAs-based SAW resonators and support their development as a scalable platform for quantum acoustics and phonon-mediated hybrid systems.
summary: This paper demonstrates high-quality GaAs-based surface acoustic wave resonators operating in the GHz range at cryogenic temperatures. By optimizing geometry and material orientation, the authors achieve high quality factors, establishing GaAs as a robust platform for phonon-mediated hybrid quantum devices.
main_problem: Achieving and characterizing high-quality (high-Q) surface acoustic wave (SAW) resonators at gigahertz frequencies and cryogenic temperatures using a Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) platform.
main_result: The researchers achieved quality factors up to 28,000 in the GHz range and demonstrated that GaAs-based SAW resonators are a viable, scalable platform for quantum acoustics with much weaker frequency-dependent loss than quartz.
method: Systematic experimental study using reflection spectroscopy via a Vector Network Analyzer to measure resonance frequency, Q-factor, and reflectivity across varying cavity lengths, wavelengths, and crystal orientations.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of GaAs-based SAW resonators acting as acoustic Fabry-Perot cavities, featuring interdigitated transducers (IDTs) and distributed Bragg reflectors (DBRs). The analysis uses a complex resonator model and a weak-reflection limit approximation for the DBRs.
key_observables: Internal quality factor (Q0), external quality factor (Qext), resonance frequency (f0), SAW velocity (v), single-finger reflectivity (|rs|), and attenuation coefficient (alpha_p).
important_parameters: Frequency range of 2.4-4-8 GHz, phonon mean free path of ~7 mm, and cavity lengths ranging from small to 200.5 lambda_0.
main_assumptions: The DBR is modeled using a weak periodic reflector approximation; the resonator is simplified to a series RLC circuit near resonance; and the system is measured in a non-vacuum environment (liquid helium).
figures_summary: Fig 1a shows reflection spectra with multiple modes; Fig 2 shows Q0 vs frequency with power-law fits; Fig 3 shows Q0 vs crystal rotation angle; Fig 4 compares reflection spectra and Q0 for different etched mesa configurations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of GHz SAW resonators, describes the GaAs experimental platform and fabrication, presents systematic results on cavity size and frequency dependence, investigates the impact of crystal orientation and structural defects (mesas), and concludes with design guidelines for quantum acoustic applications.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum acoustics and hybrid quantum systems, as it provides a scalable platform for coupling phonons to other quantum degrees of freedom (like quantum dots) at high frequencies and low temperatures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Reflection spectrum of a SAW resonator with a cavity d∗= 200.5 λ0. (b) and (c) magnitude and phase of the resonant feature highlighted in panel (a). The dashed lines show the com- plex fit used to extract the quality factor. (d) Schematics of a one- port SAW resonator using the double-electrode design for the IDT. The resonator is aligned with the [110] crystal direction. (e) Inter- nal quality factor Q0 as a function of cavity size d∗. The error bars are obtained from measurements on several devices and the red band shows a fit to Eq. 3.

FIG. 2. Internal quality factor Q0 as a function of resonance frequency f0 for devices with cavity sizes of (a) 200.5 λ0 and (b) 21.5 λ0. Error bars indicate the standard deviation of Q0 across dif- ferent resonances of the same device. Red lines show fits to Q0 ∝f c 0 over the measured frequency range, with the extracted exponents in- dicated.

FIG. 3. Internal quality factor Q0 as a function of in-plane ro- tation angle θ for devices with cavity sizes 25.5 λ0 and 200.5 λ0. Inset: angle-dependent SAW velocity extracted from the resonance frequency for d∗= 25.5 λ0. At θ = 45◦the devices show no re- sponse.

FIG. 4. (a) Reflection spectrum of SAW resonators with cavities with d∗= 200.5 λ0 with one mesa placed in one cavity (1M, in blue) and one mesa in each of the cavities (2M in red). (b) Optical micro- scope picture of a SAW device containing mesas in each cavity, false colored in purple (2M). (c) Internal quality factor for devices fabri- cated on non-etched (NE), Etched (E) GaAs, devices with a mesa in one cavity (1M) and a mesa in both cavities (2M).
